speaker: interviewer
text: --stomach was growling. You didn't pick it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: August 13, 1996. Alfred Gottschalk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's backtrack just a little bit. I want to go over your father's death, because that must have been a tremendous transition period, a tremendous impact on your life as a young man to lose your parent, your father, so early. Can you talk a little bit about what that experience was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when-- he was ill for about a year. I was 15 years old at the time. And we didn't quite know what it was. They diagnosed him with pleurisy, and pleurisy became pneumonia, and then it was an anemia, and then finally we had the-- the dreadful diagnosis. And it took him a year to wind his way out of life, which was a very difficult time for my mother, who kept working throughout that whole period. I kept going to school and working. And my father was, between hospitalizations, at home. And the hospital experience drained us of our entire savings, and we had to borrow considerable sum of money from relatives. And finally, my father came home to die. And it was a heart-wrenching experience to see this very vital man just disappear, literally, before my eyes. And the only time I saw him during this whole period-- and it must-- it was part of his illness already when it happened-- really weep was when Franklin Roosevelt died. It was as if he had lost his own father again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how reverential he was, and refugees generally were, towards Roosevelt, who was viewed as a savior, a rescuer. And the last two months of my father's life, I left school to take care of-- take care of him personally, and that was a real mitzvah, privilege, for me. And then after he died, my mother became quite ill. And thereafter, our life changed very dramatically. And I went on to college and semi-professional ball, and eventually she recovered and remarried. So it was a-- a new chapter in-- in our lives. I went on to HUC from-- from Brooklyn, and I never thought anything west of Hoboken, New Jersey existed. Hebrew Union College was quite an experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: HUC-Cincinnati.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cincinnati.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right. Just before we go to Cincinnati, you-- about the time your father died, was this when you were beginning to get the first reports of what had happened to your relatives as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: We began to make enquiry right after the war was over in 1945, through the International Red Cross, of what had happened to his parents, his sister, his brother. And there's virtually no information, until two decades later, where they then confirmed that they didn't know what happened. As I showed you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the document that we have, or documents that we have, indicated how little was actually known, even in the '50s, of what happened to individuals during the time of their incarceration in concentration camps or as prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's only recently that you've found out, for certain, some of their fates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The latest was at the United States Holocaust Memorial Council. I chaired the Academic Committee, and I brought the committee in to see our new computer and did a check on my grandparents. And within minutes, within minutes--

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the evidence came forward. We knew where they had met their death, in Minsk. Also my aunt and her husband, my father's sister and her husband, Minsk. I think I will now do a further search on the rest of the family and pretty well find out what happened, where their end came, and maybe some closure will come to this. It haunts you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --all of your thinking time as to what happened to whom and where. Because I have childhood memories of these people in my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with that knowledge, you-- as we were talking at the end of the last tape, you-- of the recent Jewish past, you moved on to your first visit to Eretz Israel, which was the new-- the Jewish future at that time. What was-- what impact did your first trip to Israel make upon you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Stanley and I came, as I told you, with rucksacks. In the rucksacks were a change of clothes and tins of tuna fish and rolls of toilet paper and a tin of coffee and a carton of cigarettes to bring to his relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through all of Europe with that. That was the gold that we watched every night to make sure that nobody would take any of that. Because, at that time in Israel, there was tremendous-- it was called [HEBREW], time of great, great shortages, where people would get two eggs a week. Milk would be measured out by-- and I saw this-- by the ladle out of cans in the streets into cups for children only. There seemed to be enough bread and an endless amount of eggplant, which you can prepare in every which way. And I can't look an eggplant in the face to this day. So we-- we ate our way through Palestine, and I lost 30 pounds in those eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Worked in kibbutzim. One was Netzer Sereni, which was composed of Holocaust survivors. At the end of the time, Stanley and I thought we had cleaned every chicken coop in Israel. Because when you go to a kibbutz to earn your way through, they say, what work can you do, what-- what can I do, or would like to do? They send you to the chicken coop. So finally when you complain about you've-- you've already shoveled enough of that stuff, they say, OK. At Netzer Sereni, they needed a barn painted inside and outside. Well, the outside was easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: The inside, I looked inside and I said, my god, there are 300-pound sacks in there of grain. And you had to move them from one side of the barn while you painted, and then you picked them up and move them to the other side of the barn. This is mind-numbing stuff. I came to Israel to be, you know, enthralled.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we left the kibbutzim, and with whatever little money we had, we began to tramp the country, hitchhike. And I almost died on the road on Tisha B'av on the ninth of Av. I didn't realize nothing moved in the country. So we were on our way from Beer-Sheva to Dimona. We got half a ride to Dimona, cut off, and then nothing moved for four hours. I'm progressively dehydrated and didn't have water bottles with us. Real greenies. [LAUGHTER] I would say.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I ended up at the Beersheba hospital for rehabilitation. Didn't mind it very much because I met a beautiful nurse there by the name of Bruria. And we just rehabilitated, and then went on, did some more hitchhiking, and saw the country and spoke to the people, worked in the fields. And that was remarkable year. It was tough to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean to you as someone who had survived the-- the Holocaust in Europe to-- to see this and to be part of this-- this new Jewish state for that-- that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, today you think of the-- the-- the Jewish state entirely differently, because so much of it is built.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '50-- when I went there, '51, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Eilat was a tank town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're kidding, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beer-Sheva, almost a tank town. Bedouins, camels, moshavim, kibbutzim, but very little settlement and very few people actually living permanently in the town. And the Negev was sparsely populated. So Haifa-- modestly populated. Tel Aviv was thronging. Jerusalem [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a small town in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't get the same feeling about Israel today that I had then, because there it was really basic. To the beggar who was sitting in the street, you know, we had no money, but you-- you give a beggar something. And the guy was sitting there and with his hand out. So I gave him a coin that I could afford to give him, and he threw it back at me and he says, what do you think I am, a beggar?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, that's-- that's chutzpah, you know, right? I'd say that's the definition.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you had all kinds of incidents like-- memorable things happened to you. And I took photographs, little Brownie, black-and-white stuff, which I look at, and it brings back a lot of memories. It was the most memorable time of my life, that visit. And when we arrived in Haifa on the ship, I remember being on deck at 4:00 in the morning and watching the sun over Mount Carmel, and that is an incomparable experience. And then when you have no money, and you have to live, you know, schnorrer your way through life, you get a different perspective. And that's why I have tremendous compassion for students who have to do it the hard way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went on from there to become a student at HUC in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Cincinnati, which you said was another world. Talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was another world in-- in every respect that I was aware of, having been both now-- having been in Israel and in New York, I came with a different mindset. And the Midwest is a world of its own, then especially. I arrived in Cincinnati [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] married. I'd since got married. And it was one of the hottest days that they had experienced that summer, and I-- my first day was in the dorm. Of course, it was not air conditioned. It was rather primitive. And gradually, we became to acculturate as more students came. But the atmosphere was one of very sophisticated, dry learning, very little emotion. And it wasn't the classical Reform that was the issue. It was that the professors were really research scholars, and the atmosphere was serious, compellingly so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were some of your professors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Professor Samuel S. Cohant taught theology, was an extraordinarily learned man, very dry. Professor Samuel Sandmill, who taught Bible and New Testament, history of Christian thought, a most erudite man. Professor Baton taught midrash and homiletics, colorful person. And Goodman taught Talmud, Schwarzman, education, Katz, human relations. These were all the old faculty that had been recruited by Dr. Morgenstern and Glueck. Now, the college had rescued [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a dozen scholars from the fires of the Holocaust. Professor Morgenstern sought out a group of scholars whom he knew he could rescue somehow. And he went to Senator Taft, who was then the-- the leading Republican congressional leader, and they got extra-maximal visas for these professors to bring them to the Cincinnati school. Among them was Abraham Joshua Herschel and the Levys, Julius and Hildegarde Levy, who were professors of Semitic languages, truly great scholars, outstanding people. And they were the nucleus for the graduate school of the college. For them, Jewish scholarship was extraordinarily serious work, and they'd make great demands on us. And that created, also, for monolingual American students, most of whom had not had serious college experience, a-- an ordeal, you know. They demanded perfection.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were the remnant, really, of the great European tradition and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of the Wissenschaft tradition. But they changed the climate of the school in very subtle ways. I remember Professor Levy was-- proclaimed himself an agnostic, and yet he would, every Shabbos, he would be at the service.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, a student said to him in class, you know, Professor Levy, I think you're a little bit of a hypocrite. Here you're proclaiming you don't believe in anything. You don't-- you're not sure. But we see you in shul every Shabbos. Isn't there a contradiction?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Julius Levy said no. You come to my class. You listen to me teach. I come to listen to you preach. And he says, it's just a courtesy. Now, with that, he covered the seriousness of his engagement with scholarship, with religion. And these-- these were extraordinary people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the president at that time was?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was-- I collated Nelson Glueck's inaugural lectures. I was an office boy at the New York school when I went to the evening college, and Nelson Glueck was then the president from 1947 to 1970. He was my predecessor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Exactly. You say Cincinnati and the Midwest were another world at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a segregated community, which was, of course, not the case in New York. And I was just reminiscing with someone yesterday who remembered me from the time when I was a student rabbi in Owensboro, Kentucky. And he was a newspaper reporter covering the integration of Lee and Sturgis, Kentucky, which were violent integrations of black kids into white schools. And I told him about how the students at HUC integrated the Busy Bee, which was the hangout for most students, when we had a black minister studying with us that year. And after a late-night study session, we went for a cup of coffee, and they wouldn't serve him, and we wouldn't order until they served him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they kept us sitting there for an hour. And finally I went to the phone, and I got all the guys in the dorm out, and we sat in the Busy Bee. We sat until they served him. And that was the end of the segregation of that restaurant. So piece by piece, you know. That was strange to us. Cincinnati was redneck, you know? And it took a while to become accustomed to it. I don't think I ever really did, to that, you know, environment. And then we began to change things here over time. You-- during your student years, you were a student rabbi at several congregations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Owensboro, Kentucky and Cleveland, Ohio.

speaker: interviewer
text: After finishing HUC, and you were ordained in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was ordained in '57. I had taught at our Towanda Camp, which was the place where entering rabbinic students spent the first segment of their studies, if they had no Hebrew backgrounds, learning Hebrew. So we went to Towanda, Pennsylvania, and I was tapped to become a member of the faculty. And Nelson Glueck invited me to go out to Los Angeles to be director of the LA school. The dean out there needed an assistant. And so I-- I persuaded my wife-- we just had had a son-- to drive west, to Indian country, you know. I'd never been to LA. I wanted to go out and see where Glueck wanted me to-- to-- direct. And he said no. We couldn't afford it. There was no money to fly me out. When I got there, I realized why he didn't want me to see it, because it was unreal. It was a little building on top of the mountain in the Hollywood Hills, accessible by mountain goat, largely. What insane people prompted the purchase of this for a school, I will never know or understand. When I got there, I literally wept.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, there were-- I was told there were 40 students, and this man needed assistance and so forth. Turned out I could only find four students, really, serious ones. They are now members of the faculty, those four--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the first four. It Michael Siner--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Louis Barth, Michael Meyer, and Barry Cogan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made it into a school. It was an exciting time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing there, and had the chance to build something out of nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, had HUC and JIR in New York joined?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That merger took place in '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were the three campuses that made up the-- the system.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three campuses, and-- and Glueck had opened a campus in '63 in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have anything to do with the Jerusalem campus at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I got there in '57, '57 to '63, there was no Jerusalem campus. There was no program. There was just archaeological work that Dr. Glueck did every-- every summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were at Los Angeles then how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that time, your daughter was also born there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Tell us the names of your children, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's name is Rachel Lisa and Marc Hillel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go after Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after Los Angeles, I came to Cincinnati. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1971. I was tapped president-designate in 1970. And when Dr. Glueck died, was-- well, he didn't die until '71. But he tapped me in '70, and he must have had a premonition of his illness. So he was going to become chancellor, and then I would be the day-to-day executive of the school. And it was a very exciting time for everyone because the LA school had just moved onto the campus of the University of Southern California. We have-- we became the first Judaica division of a private university.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And HUC developed the School of Jewish Communal Service, which was the first in the country, and did new things. And it was hard to leave there because I had not yet sat in my chair at USC when I had to come back to Cincinnati, the Midwest. And after the LA experience, it was much more difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. And during that 13 years, you completed your doctorate--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your doctrine is in what field?

speaker: interviewee
text: My doctorate is in the field of modern Zionism. I wrote on Ahad Ha'am, the founder of cultural Zionism. Doctoral dissertation was called Ahad Ha'am and the World of the Bible and the Bible Tradition, and subsequently published on Ahad Ha'am's relationship to modern Jewish history and culture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during these years, were you also beginning to develop relationships with Zionists in Eretz Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was a very formative period, because the Jerusalem school began as a school of biblical archeology. We then added the mandatory year of study in Jerusalem in 1970 for rabbis. I expanded that to include cantors, educators, and communal workers, and then progressively ordaining the first Israeli rabbi for Jerusalem, for Israel, the first woman rabbi for Israel. And now, of course, we have a thriving institution there. So it's been a very busy time this last 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And just recently you moved into a new position of service to HUC-JIR. Can you talk a little bit about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was designated chancellor of the college, which is a position that Dr. Glueck would have had had he-- had he lived. And it's a senior position of spokesperson and development work. I also want to teach, go back into the classroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I have some things I want to write and to begin to seriously to read again, you know. When you're an administrator, it's very difficult to maintain Jewish scholarship. But I did manage to publish two or three articles a year, mostly written in Israel, and a book or two. And I want to do some more serious work in the areas I have begun, namely the-- the origins development of Zionism and its-- and its changing modalities and meaning for our world today.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's stop here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got a few minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, few minutes, OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You also, during this time, have-- have served on a presidential commission of great importance to Holocaust survivors, OK? We'll begin talking about it here and then pick up on-- on the next tape more about it. Could you just start telling us about your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- in 1979, President Carter, at the instigation of Elie Wiesel, appointed a presidential commission on the Holocaust, a committee really, for the purposes of developing a memorial in the United States for the victims of the Holocaust. And part of the mandate was to travel through Eastern Europe to see memorials, to note what had been done, and then come back with a recommendation to the president. We went to Poland, started out with going to Warsaw and Treblinka, Auschwitz, then to Babi Yar, Kiev, Moscow. Then we came back via Amsterdam and then Israel, Yad Vashem. And we came back with a recommendation that no monument be erected. Because the president had in mind a monument,

speaker: interviewer
text: A statue.

speaker: interviewee
text: --statuary, you know. But that, in place of a monument, important as such, a memorial would be there be a living memorial in the form of either an archive library and museum complex or one of the above mentioned. And we came back with that as our recommendation, presented it to President Carter at the White House. And I have a photograph of that presentation. And then the Carter administration said, thank you very much, but we have no place in Washington that we can offer you. There is some land out in Virginia somewhere. And we said, give us a chance to look. So the committee delegated the responsibility to three knowledgeable people, and they found some ramshackle old buildings near the Treasury. And we told the Carter administration those are the buildings we wanted. They looked like Auschwitz. They were red brick. They had been the stables for the old Treasury. And the administration was delighted to give them to us because they knew we would come to the realization that we couldn't build in them, which took about a year or two to discover. And you couldn't do anything with them, because they were landmarked. So we said to the administration, we'll take them. And then we began the process of un-landmarking landmark buildings. That took an act of Congress.

speaker: interviewer
text: My word.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took two years, and it passed unanimously. They knocked down these ramshackle buildings, and there we were, you know, in the heart of Washington, DC. And then began the arduous process of planning and fundraising and designing the museum, which was largely designed by Holocaust survivors. There were tremendous political issues that had to be faced, because there were other victims of the Holocaust, of World War II, who wanted representation in the museum. Among them were Gypsies, the Gypsy community. There were representatives of the gay communities. There were outrageous requests by the Ukrainians, who claimed to be victims of the Holocaust. And a whole series of political involvements, which have not written-- which have not been accurately portrayed yet in the published materials, the tabletop books, that have been written about the museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you plan to write about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHTER]
